stories.title: PCCP Provides $61M to Refinance 287-Unit DC Apartment Community
stories.excerpt: PCCP provided a $61.3 million loan to PGIM and Kennedy Wilson for the refinance of Parc Riverside East, a luxury mid-rise apartment community in the Navy Yard neighborhood of Washington, D.C.Built in 2014 and located at 1011 First Street SE, the 287-unit, 13-story property features studio, one-bedroom, and two-bedroom floor plans averaging 785 square feet. “With the existing loan reaching maturity, we believe Parc Riverside East represented an opportunity for PCCP to finance a well-located, institutional-quality multifamily asset in one of Washington, D.C.’s dynamic urban neighborhoods,” said Lia Barsanti, Senior Vice President with PCCP. The property is in the heart of Washington, D.C.’s Navy Yard neighborhood with direct access to the Navy Yard–Ballpark Metro station. Nationals Park is a six-minute walk, the U.S. Capitol Building is a 17-minute walk, and the surrounding area features a mix of waterfront dining, retail, and entertainment anchored by The Yards and The Wharf. Join the industry’s top multifamily decision-makers at Connect Apartments on September 22 in L.A. Hear from the investors, developers, lenders, owners, and advisors shaping today’s apartment market through candid conversations, networking, and market insights. Register today at www.connectapts2026.com. The post PCCP Provides $61M to Refinance 287-Unit DC Apartment Community appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: PCCP refinances $61.3M for 287-unit luxury mid-rise, Parc Riverside East, Navy Yard DC
2014-built asset in walkable, transit-rich neighborhood near Metro, Nationals Park, Capitol
Maturity refi signals institutional capital confidence in DC urban multifamily despite broader market softness
stories.summary: PCCP provided a $61.3M refinance for Parc Riverside East, a 287-unit luxury mid-rise in Washington D.C.'s Navy Yard neighborhood built in 2014. The property sits in a highly amenitized waterfront submarket with direct Metro access, walkability to Nationals Park and Capitol, and proximity to The Yards and The Wharf mixed-use anchors. The maturity refinance underscores lender appetite for well-located institutional-quality urban multifamily in top metros.
stories.impact: Refi activity at Navy Yard signals sustained capital availability for premium urban product in high-barrier-to-entry DC submarkets. For developers, this validates the continued viability of transit-oriented luxury apartments in legacy urban neighborhoods, suggesting persistent demand from renters seeking walkable, entertainment-anchored locations despite moderate rent growth nationally.

stories.title: PGIM Trades Tucson-Area Assisted Living Center for $23M
stories.excerpt: The Watermark at Oro Valley, a 101-unit assisted living community at 9005 N. Oracle Road in Oro Valley, sold for $23 million. The buyer was Steller Senior Living. The seller was PGIM Real Estate. Built in 2018, the two-story assisted living facility contains 83,059 square feet on 3.42 acres in the Shadow Mountain Estates area. The property is located in Tucson’s northwest submarket near Oracle Road and Hardy Road in Oro Valley. The community includes studio, one-bedroom, and two-bedroom units, along with an on-site theater, a fitness center with daily exercise classes, a lounge, a bar, a library with a fireplace, a beauty salon and barber shop, a courtyard, and outdoor gathering spaces. The property is also near Tohono Chul Park and other recreation areas. The property will now be rebranded as The Ironwood at Oro Valley, Assisted Living & Memory Care. The post PGIM Trades Tucson-Area Assisted Living Center for $23M appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: PGIM divests 101-unit assisted living property, Oro Valley Tucson, to Steller Senior Living for $23M
2018-built asset on 3.42 acres; $23M sale equals ~$228K per unit, signaling robust pricing in senior housing
PGIM capital redeployment away from senior housing toward multifamily reflects portfolio realignment
stories.summary: PGIM Real Estate sold The Watermark at Oro Valley, a 101-unit assisted living community built in 2018, to Steller Senior Living for $23M (~$228K per unit). The property sits on 3.42 acres in Tucson's northwest submarket near Oracle Road. The asset will be rebranded as The Ironwood and remain operational as assisted living and memory care.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily impact; this is primarily a senior housing portfolio shift. However, PGIM's exit from assisted living in Tucson and redirection of capital toward multifamily (see Article 1, DC refi) suggests institutional operators are rotating away from senior care volatility and staffing challenges toward core multifamily where operational leverage and demand tailwinds remain stronger.

stories.title: Iris Escarrá of Greenberg Traurig: 5 Questions
stories.excerpt: As co-chair of the land use practice at law firm Greenberg Traurig, Iris Escarrá has worked on a number of high-profile projects. In one recent example, Escarrá was part of the team that won approvals for Miami Freedom Park, the mixed-use development near Miami International Airport that includes Nu Stadium, the 26,700-seat home of Major League Soccer’s Inter Miami CF. Before the stadium opened in April, she led the team that guided the development team through environmental remediation, code compliance and traffic and aviation issues. The following conversation has been edited for length and clarity.   Commercial Observer: Now that Nu Stadium is open and Inter Miami is playing matches there, what lessons did you learn from the project? Iris Escarrá: There’s so many. Some of them I have to cover over drinks. Some of them I can cover here. The stadium is actually one component. It’s 12 acres of a 73-acre complex that’s mixed-use. So while the stadium is the biggest to come in, we have retail coming in, hotel, office, and so forth that we’re also working on. It’s very exciting because that’s what’s actually going to create the whole district. Inter Miami has 17 home games, and they’ll have other events there, concerts, soccer games for high schools, but it’s never going to be a 365-day-a-year building. So it’s really the other uses around it that are going to create the district. And those uses around it are actually what helps with some of the traffic items because the restaurants and the retail are open a few hours before the match, so if you want to go and have lunch before the game, you could do that. If you want to go after the game, go hang out. So what happens is that the mixed-use district around the stadiums is what’s really creating the whole district. When are those other uses going to be completed? We have a few buildings that are in for permitting now, some food and beverage, some entertainment. T
bullets: Greenberg Traurig land-use counsel led approvals for Miami Freedom Park mixed-use, 73-acre complex
Nu Stadium (26,700 seats, Inter Miami CF) anchors project; retail, hotel, office components under development
Complex entitlements (environmental remediation, code, traffic, aviation) highlight regulatory burden for sports-anchored urban mixed-use
stories.summary: Iris Escarrá, co-chair of Greenberg Traurig's land-use practice, led regulatory approvals for Miami Freedom Park, a 73-acre mixed-use development near Miami International Airport. The project centers on Nu Stadium (26,700 seats, Inter Miami CF home, opened April 2025) and includes retail, hotel, and office components. Environmental remediation, code compliance, aviation, and traffic issues required extensive coordination.
stories.impact: For multifamily developers eyeing Miami, this illustrates the approval and entitlement complexity of large-scale mixed-use projects anchored by anchor tenants (sports, hospitality). South Florida growth is strong, but developers must budget extended timelines and specialized counsel for projects near airports or with environmental legacies. The precedent also shows Miami's appetite for major mixed-use infill despite regulatory friction.

stories.title: Hochul Selects Developers to Build 1,127 Housing Units in Hell’s Kitchen
stories.excerpt: A parking lot near the Intrepid Museum along the Hell’s Kitchen waterfront is about to become housing. Developers Gotham Organization, Fisher Brothers and Mural Real Estate Group have been chosen by Gov. Kathy Hochul to transform the 50,584-square-foot parking lot at 621 West 45th Street into two residential towers containing about 1,127 new homes, the governor’s office announced Wednesday. Crain’s New York Business first reported the news. Hochul announced in February of last year that she wanted to turn the state-owned parking lot along the West Side Highway — which is currently being used by the Intrepid Museum — into a residential development to help dent the city’s housing shortage. “The West Side of Manhattan has a storied history as a vibrant, inclusive community, and I’m committed to strengthening that legacy by building for a more affordable future,” Hochul said in the 2025 announcement. “By transforming this underutilized state-owned property into a dynamic mixed-use development with a significant affordable housing component, we’re taking aim at low housing supply in a high-demand area and building a stronger community for residents in Hell’s Kitchen and beyond.” Gotham, Fisher and Mural will redevelop the lot into two connected residential towers that will feature 1,127 units, with some 338 being set aside as affordable housing for those earning between 40 percent and 130 percent of the area median income, Crain’s reported. The total will include 108 for-sale condo units, with some 28 set aside as affordable housing. “Gotham has spent decades partnering with government, community organizations, and mission-driven institutions on the West Side of Manhattan to deliver housing that serves New Yorkers across the income spectrum, and this project builds on that legacy,” David Picket, CEO of Gotham, said in a statement to Commercial Observer. “This transformative development will deliver hundreds of permanently affordable homes, including homes for
bullets: NY Gov. Hochul selects Gotham, Fisher Brothers, Mural Real Estate for 1,127-unit Hell's Kitchen development
50,584 sq ft state-owned parking lot at 621 West 45th; two towers on Intrepid-adjacent West Side waterfront
Public land disposition + state entitlement bypass reduces friction; affordable-focused policy signals continued subsidy appetite in NYC
stories.summary: Gov. Hochul announced developer selection for a 1,127-unit mixed-use project on a 50,584-sf state-owned parking lot at 621 West 45th Street in Hell's Kitchen. The partnership of Gotham Organization, Fisher Brothers, and Mural Real Estate will build two residential towers on the Intrepid-adjacent West Side Highway waterfront. The project aligns with Hochul's affordable-housing mandate and February 2025 state land-release commitment.
stories.impact: State-owned land disposition in Manhattan eliminates acquisition risk and streamlines entitlements, reducing developer capital deployment and timeline friction. This signals continued New York subsidy and land-grant strategy to drive supply in high-barrier submarkets. For competitive developers, public land deals create below-market basis but entangle entitlements with political cycles. The 1,127-unit scale suggests belief in Hell's Kitchen demand recovery post-COVID and positions the submarket for rental supply growth, potentially pressuring rents near West Side Highway.

stories.title: Jewish High School Championed by Jeff Sutton Secures $30M in Construction Financing
stories.excerpt: A Jewish school in Brooklyn’s Gravesend neighborhood has received approval from the state attorney general for a $30 million construction loan, Commercial Observer has learned. Magen David Yeshivah, which operates multiple day schools in Gravesend, secured the $30 million loan from BankUnited for the continued construction of a new Sephardic high school along McDonald Avenue. The floating-rate, three-year loan agreement mortgages Magen David’s nearby elementary school property at 2130 McDonald Avenue. Religious corporations in New York must have approval from the state attorney general or a supreme court justice before mortgaging, selling or leasing most real estate. The school acquired its future home across three sites — 1905, 1911 and 1915 McDonald Avenue — in 2023 for $13.5 million. Jeff Sutton, a prominent retail landlord and an active Magen David trustee, assisted the school in the deal, The Real Deal reported at the time. It is unclear whether Sutton, or any brokers, lent their expertise in arranging the new loan terms. A diagram of a planned school at 370 Quentin Road, Brooklyn. DIAGRAM: Sherida Paulsen Architect via NYC Department of Buildings Plans for the $50 million high school span 81,000 square feet, PincusCo first reported. Remaining construction costs total $37.46 million, and the building is slated for completion in early 2027. Magen David school, founded in 1946, sold its former high school at 7801 Bay Parkway to Success Academy Charter Schools for $37.2 million in 2024, according to TRD. It continues to operate an early childhood center at 2170 McDonald Avenue and its nearby elementary school. Spokespeople for Magen David and BankUnited did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Magen David Yeshivah secures $30M floating-rate construction loan, BankUnited, for Gravesend, Brooklyn high school
Loan mortgages existing elementary school at 2130 McDonald Ave; school acquired future sites (1905, 1911, 1915 McDonald) in 2023 for $13.5M
Religious-sector construction financing signals institutional bank appetite for faith-based real estate outside traditional multifamily/office
stories.summary: Magen David Yeshivah secured a $30M floating-rate, three-year construction loan from BankUnited to build a new Sephardic high school in Brooklyn's Gravesend neighborhood along McDonald Avenue. The loan is secured by the school's existing elementary school property. The organization acquired the future development sites (three parcels) in 2023 for $13.5M, with the total project estimated at $50M+. Prominent retail landlord Jeff Sutton served as an active trustee.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily relevance. However, the $30M deployment into Gravesend reflects broader Brooklyn capital activity and institutional bank appetite for community-anchored projects. For multifamily developers in South Brooklyn, rising institutional investment in schools and community assets signals neighborhood stabilization and potential demographic tailwinds (family formation, school proximity). The deal also underscores floating-rate construction financing availability despite recent rate volatility.

stories.title: L.A. County Property Values Hit $2.27 Trillion Despite Wildfires, Sluggish Market
stories.excerpt: Los Angeles County’s taxable property values reached a record $2.272 trillion at the start of 2026, rising 4.42 percent year-over-year despite the devastating wildfires 17 months ago, and despite a real estate market that in some ways is still recovering from the COVID pandemic. The assessment roll reflects property values as of Jan. 1, and it helps government officials prepare annual budgets and forecast tax revenue. The latest assessment roll includes nearly 2.4 million taxable parcels. L.A. County Assessor Jeff Prang announced that the assessment roll grew by $96 billion from 2025, making it the 16th straight year of value growth. The roll is expected to generate more than $27 billion in property taxes for public schools, health care, public safety and other government services. “When I presented the forecast to the Board of Supervisors in May, I emphasized 2025-2026 was a challenging year, and the impact of [last] January’s devastating wildfires will be felt for years to come,” Prang said in a statement. “Even so, Los Angeles County’s economy continues to demonstrate remarkable resilience.” Property transfers led the way by adding $49 billion in assessed value, while new construction contributed more than $12 billion. The maximum 2 percent inflation adjustment permitted under state law added another $43 billion. L.A. County home sales moderated during 2025, but the median home sale price still reached $982,000. The city of Los Angeles posted the highest assessed value at $926.1 billion. Long Beach hit $84.5 billion, and Santa Monica reached $54.9 billion. Irwindale’s assessed value jumped 15 percent for the most gain over the year, followed by Vernon at 13.3 percent, and Hidden Hills at 11.7 percent. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: LA County assessed property values reach $2.272T, +4.42% YoY, despite January 2025 wildfires and COVID-recovery drag
16-year consecutive value growth; assessment roll supports $27B+ in property tax revenue for schools, services
Resilience despite macro headwinds signals underlying demand strength and limited distress in core LA markets
stories.summary: Los Angeles County's taxable property values reached $2.272T as of January 1, 2026, up 4.42% year-over-year and marking the 16th consecutive year of growth. The assessment roll includes 2.4M taxable parcels and grew $96B from 2025, generating an estimated $27B+ in property tax revenue. Despite the devastating January 2025 wildfires and lingering COVID-recovery effects, the market demonstrated resilience.
stories.impact: LA County's sustained value growth despite wildfire damage and sluggish transaction volume underscores strong underlying demand in core submarkets and resistance to downward revaluations. For multifamily developers, this signals that Class A, well-located assets in non-fire-affected zones retain pricing power and lender appetite. However, the 4.42% growth lags historical pre-pandemic and early post-pandemic norms, confirming the moderation in rent and value growth flagged across the industry—counsel patience on underwriting assumptions rather than aggressive expansion into secondary LA geographies.

stories.title: Boeing Adding to Mesa Manufacturing Facility
stories.excerpt: A Mesa planning board will consider a nearly 268,000 proposed expansion to an existing Boeing manufacturing facility on the east side of Greenfield Road north of McDowell Road. (Photo courtesy of Todd Photographic Services) AZBEX reports the one-story building is proposed as a Boeing Fabrication Center, adjacent to an existing Boeing Fabrication Center totaling 158,000 square feet, and will be used “for fabrication of highly specialized carbon military parts. (Photo courtesy of Todd Photographic Services) Boeing’s Arizona presence is anchored in Mesa, where its campus has served for decades as the home of AH-64 Apache helicopter production and advanced aerospace manufacturing. Boeing employs more than 4,500 Arizonans, works with more than 270 suppliers and vendors statewide, supports an estimated 49,000 direct and indirect jobs and generated $1.1 billion in supplier spending in 2025. Beyond Mesa, Boeing’s Arizona operations extend to Chandler and Goodyear, supporting a broad aerospace value chain, including manufacturing, logistics, supply chain management and engineering. Connect Texas Multifamily in Dallas on August 13 brings together the owners, developers, brokers, investors, and lenders shaping the future of Texas multifamily for a day of market intelligence, networking, and actionable insights. Join 300+ decision-makers and be part of the conversations driving the Texas multifamily market. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post Boeing Adding to Mesa Manufacturing Facility appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Boeing expanding Mesa manufacturing facility by 268,000 sq ft for carbon military parts fabrication
Boeing employs 4,500+ Arizonans, supports ~49,000 direct/indirect jobs, $1.1B supplier spending (2025)
Aerospace hub consolidation in Phoenix metro (Mesa, Chandler, Goodyear) signals sustained job and demand tailwinds
stories.summary: Boeing is proposing a 268,000-sf expansion to its existing fabrication center in Mesa, Arizona, adding a new one-story facility for specialized carbon military parts. Boeing's Arizona footprint spans Mesa (AH-64 Apache production), Chandler, and Goodyear, employing 4,500+ workers, supporting ~49,000 direct/indirect jobs, and generating $1.1B in supplier spending in 2025.
stories.impact: Boeing's Mesa expansion reinforces Phoenix metro as a durable aerospace and manufacturing hub, anchoring sustained employment and wage growth. For multifamily developers across Phoenix submarkets (Chandler, Mesa, Goodyear, North Scottsdale), this signals multi-year job stability and in-migration pressure from highly skilled aerospace workers and suppliers—validating new apartment supply pipelines in these tech-adjacent corridors. The facility expansion also supports industrial real estate strength in East Valley.

stories.title: Thorofare Capital CEO Kevin Miller Exits Company After 17 Years
stories.excerpt: One year after losing its three head originators, Thorofare Capital’s shakeup is now evident at the very top of the C-suite level. Kevin Miller, who spent the last nearly 17 years as Thorofare’s CEO, is exiting the L.A.-based lender, according to sources familiar with the departure and an internal memo shared with Commercial Observer. “We thank Kevin for founding the firm and for his long-tenured service and role in the firm’s growth and success,” the July 15 memo sent to Thorofare limited partners said. The circumstances surrounding Miller’s departure were not immediately known, nor was the timetable for naming a replacement. Miller did not immediately return requests for comment. The loss of Miller comes on the heels of another big leadership hit Thorofare took last year culminating with the company parting ways with Felix Gutnikov, h​​ead of originations, in July 2025. Brendan Miller, Thorofare’s president and chief investment officer, told CO at the time the move was part of the company’s strategy to head in a “new direction” toward a “regional-focused origination model.” Gutnikov was let go by Thorofare soon after David Perlman decided to leave his role leading the firm’s New York City office in early 2025 to relocate to Auckland, New Zealand. Thorofare also saw Jonathan Hart, head of the lender’s Dallas office , leave for S3 Capital in June 2025. It seems that Brendan Miller will now lead the company in Kevin Miller’s absence. “The management team led by Brendan Miller and the broader organization remain well positioned to continue executing the fund’s investment strategy and to serve its investors, maintaining the disciplined approach that has guided the firm throughout its 16-year history,” the July memo stated. “We appreciate your continued confidence and partnership and are very excited about the future of the firm.” Brendan Miller did not immediately return a request for comment. Thorofare saw large-sale growth under Kevin Miller’s leadership,
bullets: Thorofare Capital CEO Kevin Miller exits after 17 years; follows loss of three head originators in 2025
LA-based debt lender undergoing leadership restructure; internal memo indicates strategic pivot to 'regional-focused origination'
Leadership instability and originator departures signal lender consolidation and tightening in construction/acquisition financing market
stories.summary: Kevin Miller, founder and CEO of LA-based lender Thorofare Capital for 17 years, has exited the firm. His departure follows the July 2025 loss of three head originators, including Felix Gutnikov (head of originations). An internal memo to limited partners cited the need to pivot toward a 'regional-focused origination' strategy, signaling strategic realignment.
stories.impact: Thorofare's instability and leadership exodus reflect broader consolidation and stress in the independent construction and multifamily debt market. For developers, this underscores the need to diversify lender relationships and avoid over-reliance on regional or specialty lenders facing origination headwinds. The pivot toward 'regional focus' suggests retreat from national origination—tightening the non-agency debt availability landscape and raising financing costs/terms for off-market or non-agency debt structures.

stories.title: StreetLights Advancing Apartment Venture at Former Plano Office Campus
stories.excerpt: StreetLights Residential has secured $54 million in equity, along with $111.5 million in construction loans, for a new 22-story Plano apartment complex expected to open in 2029. The project will be part of the conversion of The Park of Legacy, a 107-acre office campus into a mixed-use development with apartments, hotels and restaurants. It used to be JCPenney’s headquarters. Northmarq’s Dallas debt and equity team — Joel Heikenfeld, Charlie Rimkus and Charlie Robinson — arranged the equity financing on behalf of StreetLights. The transaction is structured on a five-year term. Residence sizes will range from 682 to 2,843 square feet. The project will also build three 3-bedroom townhomes ranging from 3,283 to 3,316 square feet next to the high-rise. Amenities will include a pool with private cabanas, a sky lounge with panoramic views, a golf simulator, a fitness center and coworking spaces. Residents will also have access to a library and will have pet-friendly features. Connect Texas Multifamily in Dallas on August 13 brings together the owners, developers, brokers, investors, and lenders shaping the future of Texas multifamily for a day of market intelligence, networking, and actionable insights. Join 300+ decision-makers and be part of the conversations driving the Texas multifamily market. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post StreetLights Advancing Apartment Venture at Former Plano Office Campus appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: StreetLights Residential secures $54M equity + $111.5M construction loans for 22-story Plano apartment tower, opening 2029
Project converts 107-acre office campus (former JCPenney HQ) into mixed-use with apartments, hotels, restaurants
Office-to-residential conversion trend; Northmarq debt/equity arrange signals strong equity appetite for trophy Plano infill despite office headwinds
stories.summary: StreetLights Residential secured $54M in equity and $111.5M in construction loans for a 22-story apartment tower at The Park of Legacy, a 107-acre former JCPenney headquarters in Plano. The project, opening in 2029, includes 682–2,843 sf units and three townhomes, with amenities including a sky lounge, golf simulator, and coworking spaces. Northmarq arranged equity and debt on a five-year term.
stories.impact: The Park of Legacy conversion exemplifies Dallas's shift toward office-to-residential adaptive use in suburban office campuses facing secular demand headwinds. Strong equity commitment ($54M) signals investor confidence in Plano's talent/demographic appeal and mixed-use amenity synergies (hotel, restaurant). For developers, this validates the Plano submarket's ability to attract trophy institutional capital and demonstrates the viability of large-format office site conversions in supply-constrained Texas metros—expect similar legacy office-park redevelopment as office demand remains structurally challenged.

stories.title: Private Equity Turns to Debt Investments to Add Multifamily Assets
stories.excerpt: U.S. multifamily advertised rents increased by an average of $4 in June to $1,763, continuing the recent trend of moderate growth, Yardi Matrix reported. Rents rose 0.7% during the second quarter of 2026 and 1.0% in the first half of the year. Both metrics fall well below the pace seen in both the immediate pre-pandemic period and the first few post-pandemic years. However, Yardi Matrix reported, slow rent growth has not stopped investors from allotting capital to the sector. At the same time, growth in property sales has been weak, leading private equity to book multifamily through debt investments at differing levels of the capital stack. “Debt funds are grabbing a growing share of the pie, agency lenders Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac have expanded their securitization offerings, and pooled multifamily-only CMBS deals are making a comeback for the first time since the 2008 financial crisis,” reported Yardi Matrix. Stay ahead of the multifamily market at Connect Apartments. Gain firsthand insights from industry leaders on capital markets, development, operations, financing, and investment trends while expanding your network with the people making deals happen. See you on September 22! The post Private Equity Turns to Debt Investments to Add Multifamily Assets appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: US multifamily rents rise $4 in June to $1,763 avg; +0.7% Q2, +1.0% H1 2026—well below pre-pandemic/early post-pandemic pace
Weak property sales drive PE into debt investments at varying capital stack levels; debt funds, agency CMBS, multifamily-only CMBS surge
Capital abundant but equity deal flow constrained; debt markets filling void with risk-shifting implications
stories.summary: U.S. multifamily rents averaged $1,763 in June 2026 (+$4 month-over-month), posting only +0.7% Q2 and +1.0% H1 growth—a significant deceleration from pre-pandemic and early post-pandemic norms. Despite sluggish rent growth and weak property sales, capital continues flowing into the sector. Private equity, unable to access equity deals, is increasingly allocating capital to debt at various capital-stack levels, with debt funds expanding, agency securitization growing, and pooled multifamily CMBS returning for the first time since 2008.
stories.impact: Rent growth moderation is structural, not cyclical—signaling oversupply in many markets and slower per-unit underwriting assumptions for new development. However, the surge of PE into debt (mezzanine, preferred equity, first-loss) reveals capital abundance seeking yield and points to rising leverage in the capital stack. For developers, this signals continued debt availability but higher pricing and more aggressive risk retention by lenders. Expect continued pressure on equity returns and valuation compression, particularly in non-gateway and secondary markets. The CMBS revival also signals perceived stabilization and institutional appetite for portfolio sales and securitization—opening refinance and portfolio exit windows.

stories.title: PTM Partners, Peacock launch subsidy-free workforce housing platform
stories.excerpt: Florida-based PTM Partners and Peacock Capital launched Inception Housing, a platform designed to deliver lower-cost housing without relying on government aid.  The venture targets middle-income renters earning between 80 and 120 percent of the area median income, cutting development costs by handling much of the design, construction and material procurement in-house, according to a news release.  By using a standardized building design and cold-formed steel purchased directly from suppliers, Inception Housing can cut hard construction costs by about 25 percent compared to similar products, PTM Partners CEO Michael Tillman said. The platform’s strategy delivers buildings in six to seven months, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: PTM Partners, Peacock Capital launch Inception Housing for subsidy-free workforce housing (80–120% AMI) in South Florida
Cold-formed steel, standardized design, in-house procurement cut hard costs ~25%; 6–7 month delivery vs. 12–14 month traditional
South Florida needs 30,460+ units; Inception targets middle-income renters priced out of market; one project (Magnolia Flats) underway in Orlando
stories.summary: Florida-based PTM Partners and Peacock Capital launched Inception Housing, a platform delivering workforce housing (80–120% AMI) without subsidies. Using standardized design, cold-formed steel, and direct supplier procurement, Inception cuts hard construction costs ~25% and delivery time to 6–7 months (vs. 12–14 traditional). South Florida faces a 30,460-unit shortage; statewide, 120,850 units. Inception's first project, Magnolia Flats (Orlando), adds 74 units to existing townhomes.
stories.impact: Inception's cost and delivery compression model addresses the affordability/supply pinch in Florida by removing government subsidy dependency and accelerating time-to-stabilization. The 25% cost reduction and 50% faster delivery model—if scalable—reshapes underwriting and pro formas in competitive Sun Belt metros. For developers, this signals rising competitive pressure from cost-engineering platforms targeting workforce housing. The focus on middle-income (80–120% AMI) renters highlights a supply gap between subsidized and market-rate, validating the viability of low-margin, high-velocity development. Live Local Act incentives suggest political support for this model.

stories.title: Dallas apartment pipeline gets a boost with pair of financing deals
stories.excerpt: Two developers of a pair of apartment complexes in the Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex recently secured financing for their separate projects.  Endeavor Real Estate locked in financing for their luxury high-rise at 2700 McKinney Avenue named Lucille. Additionally, StreetLights Residential snagged $54 million in equity financing for an apartment tower in Plano, Texas, which will be built in the redevelopment of The Park at Legacy at 6501 Legacy Drive.  Lucille will feature 265 units across 22 stories in Uptown, according to the Dallas Morning News. The development is helmed by Austin-based Endeavor and equity partner Canyon Partners Real Estate. Construction on […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Endeavor secures financing for 265-unit Lucille tower (22-story) at 2700 McKinney, Dallas Uptown; opens late 2028
StreetLights lands $54M equity for Plano Park of Legacy conversion; both deals signal strong Dallas multifamily debt/equity appetite
Uptown and suburban Plano both moving forward in same financing cycle—bifurcated demand across urban core and redeveloped office corridors
stories.summary: Endeavor Real Estate and equity partner Canyon Partners secured financing for Lucille, a 265-unit, 22-story luxury high-rise at 2700 McKinney in Dallas Uptown, targeting opening in late 2028. The 320,000-sf project includes 10,000 sf ground-floor retail, rooftop pool, observation deck, and 4-story below-grade parking. Simultaneously, StreetLights Residential closed $54M equity for its Plano tower conversion (see Article 9). Both deals underscore strong Dallas multifamily financing availability.
stories.impact: Two major Dallas financed starts in tandem signal bifurcated growth: urban-core Uptown luxury (targeting working professionals in financial district) and suburban office-conversion mixed-use. Capital is flowing to both infill urbanized submarkets and large-format office-to-residential plays, validating Dallas's two-track supply strategy. For developers, this shows sustained lender and equity confidence across risk profiles—both trophy urban infill and suburban adaptive-use can access top-tier capital, supporting aggressive supply growth in DFW.

stories.title: Texas’ top construction permits for the week ending July 14, 2026
stories.excerpt: New Construction Fort Worth’s public hospital John Peter Smith Hospital filed the state’s biggest construction project of the week to build a $300 million central utility plant at 1575 South Main Street on Fort Worth’s South Side. Construction on the 113,000-square-foot facility started at the end of June and is expected to wrap up in May 2028.  Job filing number: TABS2026024842 Houston Independent School District filed plans to build a 216,000-square-foot career and technical center at 9220 Jutland Road in Houston. Construction on the project, which will feature classrooms, labs, a multipurpose center, kitchen and administration spaces, is expected to […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Fort Worth JPS Hospital $300M central utility plant; HISD $216K career/tech center; Amazon $98M Terrell distribution center—all under construction
Dallas Museum of Art $20M mechanical/fire system upgrade; Austin tech TI $14M at Shoal Creek Walk office
Mix of healthcare, education, logistics, and commercial office TI signals broad-based construction activity beyond residential
stories.summary: Texas construction permits for week ending July 14, 2026 include: Fort Worth JPS Hospital $300M central utility plant (113,000 sf, completion May 2028); Houston ISD $216,000-sf career/technical center ($9.2M Jutland Rd, completion Sept 2028); Amazon $98M, 1.2M-sf distribution center in Terrell; Dallas Museum of Art $20M mechanical upgrades; Austin tech TI $14M (Shoal Creek Walk).
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. However, the breadth and scale of healthcare, education, logistics, and office construction underscores sustained non-residential capital deployment in Texas metro markets. For multifamily developers, this signals strong employment growth drivers (hospital expansions, career/tech training, logistics hub activity) that support long-term demand. Amazon's 1.2M-sf Terrell distribution center and HISD's career-tech center point to sustained job creation and workforce skill development—validating multifamily supply in logistics-adjacent (Terrell, DFW corridor) and education-adjacent nodes.

stories.title: Dolphins out, superyachts in: David Martin’s marina plan at former Seaquarium getting underway
stories.excerpt: The site of the former Miami Seaquarium on Virginia Key is headed for a major redevelopment.  Miami-Dade County and Terra Group’s David Martin want to trade dolphin shows for superyachts with an application to build what could be the largest marina in the county, the Miami Herald reported.  The application, submitted to state regulators, calls for 325 boat slips, about 90 of which could accommodate yachts longer than 80 feet, and a 500-boat dry stack, the outlet said. The plan includes a fuel depot, a network of floating docks that extends halfway across the channel separating Virginia Key from nearby […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Terra Group's David Martin negotiates Miami-Dade lease for former Seaquarium redevelopment; 325 boat slips + 500-unit dry stack marina
FDEP raises wetlands, coral, water-quality concerns; complex environmental/regulatory approvals required; Martin receives 95% of boat-slip profits
Waterfront mixed-use (superyacht marina) signals premium amenity-led redevelopment strategy; regulatory complexity typical of Miami waterfront projects
stories.summary: Terra Group's David Martin is negotiating a ground lease with Miami-Dade County to redevelop the former Miami Seaquarium site (4400 Rickenbacker Causeway, Virginia Key) into a large marina. The plan includes 325 boat slips (~90 for 80+ ft yachts), 500-unit dry stack, fuel depot, floating docks, wave break, and 19,000-sf fuel dock. Martin retains 95% of boat-slip profit. Florida DEP has raised concerns on wetlands, coral, and water quality; dredging and design alternatives under review.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact; this is a luxury marine/hospitality play. However, the Seaquarium redevelopment precedent signals Miami-Dade's shift toward high-margin waterfront amenity-led mixed-use (superyacht focus vs. traditional recreation) and the complex regulatory/environmental approval process for major Virginia Key/Biscayne projects. For multifamily developers, this highlights the multi-year entitlement timeline and environmental burden of Miami waterfront sites—counsel patience and robust environmental/agency budgeting for any Biscayne or Virginia Key-adjacent projects.

stories.title: TIF funding for the 78 gets key approval for $425M for needed infrastructure
stories.excerpt: The Chicago City Council Committee on Finance approved a deal to fund $425 million of infrastructure at the 78, a mixed-use development that will include a privately-funded new stadium for the Major League Soccer franchise the Chicago Fire.  The deal would repay developer Related Midwest for project-supporting structures using tax increment financing money, including $287 million in TIF money from a nearby district. The next step in the deal will be getting the formal green light from the City Council, who are set to hold a final vote on the agreement on Wednesday, according to Crain’s.  Joe Mansueto, the Fire’s […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Chicago City Council approves $425M TIF funding for the 78 mixed-use; $287M from Canal/Congress TIF district redirected to Roosevelt/Clark TIF
Related Midwest receives TIF reimbursement for infrastructure supporting privately-funded MLS stadium (Chicago Fire) + retail/office
Public TIF reallocation reduces developer cash deployment burden; reflects Chicago's subsidy-led development model for anchor projects
stories.summary: Chicago City Council Finance Committee approved $425M in TIF funding for the 78, a mixed-use development anchored by a privately-funded MLS stadium for the Chicago Fire. The deal redirects $287M from the established Canal/Congress TIF district to the Roosevelt/Clark TIF district (where the 78 sits), providing Related Midwest with infrastructure reimbursement. A formal City Council vote is set for Wednesday. Alderman Bill Conway expressed concern over draining TIF funds from Union Station and transit investments in his district.
stories.impact: TIF reallocation accelerates the 78's infrastructure deployment and reduces Related Midwest's near-term capital burden, lowering equity return requirements and cash flow pressure. For developers, Chicago's subsidy-led anchor project model (sports stadium + mixed-use) shows continued willingness to deploy public capital if a marquee tenant (MLS, owner Joe Mansueto) is secured. However, the TIF shift signals fiscal tension: reallocating from an established district (Canal/Congress) to a new project raises questions about long-term TIF district sustainability and opportunity cost. Expect continued political debate over TIF allocation as the city balances growth (the 78) vs. legacy infrastructure (transit, Union Station).

stories.title: Housing Notes: Developers are in on the joke: Apologize later
stories.excerpt: I have long had a fascination with the grit it takes to be a Manhattan developer. After all, I taught market analysis to a class of approximately 150 graduate students in Columbia’s Master of Science in Real Estate Development (MSRED) program for 8 years. My students have ended up all over the U.S. and hopefully they learned not to accept “no” as an answer when they’re out in the world. That’s the dividing line since developers tend to ignore the word “no” and keep pushing, when non-developers like me would cower in fear and self-doubt. It helps to look at […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: NY development legend Harry Macklowe pursued midnight demolition of NYC building; author reflects on developer psychology and risk tolerance
Nathan Berman's MetroLoft (former Pfizer HQ office-to-residential conversion) suffered partial structural collapse—reputational and project risk
Development culture commentary: developers push regulatory boundaries, accept failure, restart; structural/liability risks rising with conversion complexity
stories.summary: The article reflects on the psychology and business practices of Manhattan developers, examining Harry Macklowe's controversial midnight demolition tactics and contrasting them with the quieter approach of Nathan Berman (MetroLoft), whose Pfizer HQ office-to-residential conversion recently suffered a partial structural collapse. The author notes that developers typically operate with high-risk tolerance, ignore regulatory constraints, and often recover after failures—a cultural pattern distinct from other real estate professionals.
stories.impact: Peripheral relevance to multifamily development but illustrative of execution risk in large-scale Manhattan office-to-residential conversions. Berman's structural failure at the Pfizer HQ conversion—a marquee Lower Manhattan project—signals that even high-pedigree, well-capitalized developers face material construction, engineering, and liability risks in complex conversion work. For developers considering NYC office conversions, this underscores the need for robust structural engineering vetting, phased delivery, and insurance/liability reserves. The anecdote also highlights regulatory enforcement variability in NYC (Macklowe's midnight demolition tactics) and suggests that aggressive entitlement strategies carry reputational and legal tail risk.
